g.text: Work Efficiency Tradeoff | "Sequential O(n) vs Parallel O(n log n)"
Parallel: Touch elements at each tree level

div.p: Step 1: a₀ + a₁ = s₁ Step 2: s₁ + a₂ = s₂ Step 3: s₂ + a₃ = s₃ ... Step n-1: sₙ₋₂ + aₙ₋₁ = result

g.text: Elements touched multiple times across levels

div.p: Level 0 (Input): a₀ a₁ a₂ a₃ a₄ a₅ a₆ a₇ Level 1: └a₀+a₁┘ └a₂+a₃┘ └a₄+a₅┘ └a₆+a₇┘ Level 2: └─sum₀+sum₁─┘ └─sum₂+sum₃─┘ Level 3: └─────final sum─────┘
Key Insight: Parallel algorithms trade total work for reduced latency. More processors working simultaneously completes faster even though each processor does less-efficient work.
Time │ │ ╱ Sequential (1 proc) │ ╱ │ ╱ │ ╱ ╱ Parallel (p procs) │ ╱ ╱ │╱ ╱ └─────────────────── n ↑ Crossover
Below crossover: Overhead exceeds benefit Above crossover: Parallelism wins despite extra work Crossover depends on: • Number of processors (p) • Overhead per parallel step • Problem size (n)

Step Complexity (Depth/Span): How many sequential steps with infinite processors? → Parallel reduction: O(log n) steps Work Complexity: Total operations across all processors? → Parallel reduction: O(n log n) work (not work-efficient!) Work-Efficient Algorithm: Does no more work than sequential → Blelloch scan: O(n) work in O(log n) steps ✓

g.text: Parallelism ≠ Less Work | Parallelism = Lower Latency
